title: Empirical Network Contagion for U.S. Financial Institutions

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: This Federal Reserve Bank of New York staff paper (SR 826, revised October 2019) constructs an empirical measure of expected network spillovers through default cascades for the U.S. financial system covering 2002-2016. The study uses actual balance sheet exposures across bank holding companies, broker-dealers, and insurance companies, finding negligible spillovers during 2002-2007 and 2013-2016, but significant spillovers during 2008-2012 where default cascades amplified expected losses by up to 25 percent.
key_points: Covers U.S. financial system 2002-2016, including all bank holding companies, broker-dealers, and insurance companies
Uses actual empirical balance sheet exposures rather than simulations or single-market instruments
Found negligible expected spillovers during tranquil periods: 2002-2007 and 2013-2016
Found significant spillovers during stress period 2008-2012, amplifying expected losses by up to 25%
Higher contagion estimate than previously found in literature
why_it_matters: This research provides empirical estimates of systemic risk through network contagion, showing how defaults can cascade through the financial system. The 25% loss amplification figure during the 2008-2012 period offers a quantifiable benchmark for understanding tail risk in interconnected financial networks. Regulators and risk managers can use this to assess how counterparty exposures might amplify losses during periods of financial stress.
market_structure_context: This paper directly addresses market structure by examining network topology and interconnections among U.S. financial institutions. It quantifies how the structure of financial networks (counterparty exposures across banks, broker-dealers, and insurers) affects systemic risk through default cascades.
